title: Bitbucket の 404 エラー：原因と解決策
reasons: The article contains a critical factual inaccuracy regarding the recommendation of App Passwords for scripts and pipelines, as Bitbucket is deprecating them in favor of API Tokens.
Some claims, while generally true by common convention, lack explicit official documentation for precise wording or examples (e.g., exact JSON error formats, #123 PR format in UI).
one or more claims are unsupported or unverifiable
required_actions: Update the section on 'App Passwords' to reflect their deprecation and recommend using API Tokens instead, including relevant permissions and migration guidance.
Add explicit citations to official Bitbucket documentation or relevant Git documentation for all factual claims.
Clarify the context of 'personal workspaces' and their workspace ID (username match) to reflect changes in Bitbucket Cloud's behavior as of February 2023.
Add official or primary source URLs for unsupported claims.

sources.title: Fix Git's 'fatal: repository not found' error quickly - TheServerSide
sources.claim: This error occurs during API calls, Git operations, or when clicking links in the Web UI, and is usually caused by incorrect resource paths or slugs, or by the authenticated user lacking access rights. And Example Git error: fatal: repository 'https://bitbucket.org/my-workspace/wrong-repo.git/' not found. And Authentication is required to access private repositories. And If access is attempted without authentication, or with credentials that lack read permissions, Bitbucket may respond with a 404 error. This is for security reasons. And If a repository is set to 'private', unauthenticated access is often automatically rejected with a 404.

sources.title: Bitbucket Server or Data Center displays a 404 error when opening a repository
sources.claim: Bitbucket 404 error indicates that the specified repository or resource (branch, commit, pull request, etc.) cannot be found. And This error occurs during API calls, Git operations, or when clicking links in the Web UI, and is usually caused by incorrect resource paths or slugs, or by the authenticated user lacking access rights. And In Bitbucket, the URL name of a repository is called a 'slug'. And If this slug is incorrectly written in an API URL or Git command, a 404 error will occur. And If access is attempted without authentication, or with credentials that lack read permissions, Bitbucket may respond with a 404 error. This is for security reasons. And If a repository is set to 'private', unauthenticated access is often automatically rejected with a 404.

unsupported_claims: The article implicitly claims that the JSON error format {"type": "error", "error": {"message": "..."}} is standard for Bitbucket Cloud API 404 responses. While commonly encountered by users, an explicit official documentation stating this exact structure as the standard for all 404 scenarios was not found.

The article implicitly claims that curl output for Bitbucket API 404s always follows the exact JSON structure: {"type": "error", "error": {"message": "Resource not found"}}. While the 'Resource not found' message is common, the precise JSON structure is not universally documented as the official standard.

The claim that a pull request number is displayed as '#123' in the Web UI is a common convention across many Git platforms. However, explicit official Bitbucket documentation or screenshots confirming this exact '#123' format were not found in the search results.

The claim that for individual accounts, the workspace ID is the same as the username needs more nuance. While this was true by default for legacy personal workspaces, new users cannot create personal workspaces with the username as the workspace ID automatically as of February 2023. Shared workspaces require unique IDs distinct from usernames.
